Facts.text: Battle of Qatwan [Samarkand]: Seljuqs are defeated by a Kara-Khitan army, signaling the beginning of the end of the Great Seljuk Empire
Henry II, Duke of Austria, leaves Bavaria
Statute of Kalisz is promulgated by Boleslaus the Pious, Duke of Greater Poland, guaranteeing Jews safety and personal liberties and granting battei din jurisdiction over Jewish matters
John XXI [Peter Juliani] is elected Pope, the only Portuguese Pope
Stefan Dušan declares himself King of Serbia
Battle of Kulikovo: Moscow's great monarch Dmitry defeats the Mongols, beginning the decline of the Tatars
Battle of Tumu Fortress: Mongolians capture the Chinese Emperor Yingzong of Ming
Battle of Orsha: Polish-Lithuanian army defeats the Russian army
Earl of Hertford leads a retaliatory mission against Scotland
Maximilian is chosen king of Hungary
First permanent European settlement in the US is founded in St. Augustine, Florida
Turkish siege of Malta is broken by the Maltese and the Knights of St. John
Dutch surrender colony of New Netherland, including New York, to 300 English soldiers
China and Russia sign the Treaty of Nerchinsk, checking Russian expansion but opening trade further between the two countries
Pope Clement XI publishes the decree "Unigenitus" against Jansenism
A barn fire during a puppet show in the village of Burwell in Cambridgeshire, England, kills 78 people, many of whom are children
Battle of Lake George in the Province of New York: British army defeats French
Kittanning Expedition: 30-40 Lenape Indians are killed by Pennsylvania Provincial troops during the French and Indian War
French army surrenders Montreal to British commander General Jeffrey Amherst
Mission San Gabriel Arcángel is founded in California, just east of modern downtown Los Aangeles
William IV is crowned King of Great Britain at the age of 64, becoming the oldest person to assume the British throne
The US under General Scott defeats the Mexicans at the Battle of Molino del Rey
British and French troops capture Sevastopol from the Russians, effectively ending the Crimean War
French forces successfully assault the Malakoff Line during the Crimean War
Excursion steamer "Lady Elgin" sinks after being rammed in a storm on Lake Michigan, drowning about 300, the largest loss of life on the Great Lakes
Battle of Sabine Pass, Texas: 47 Texas volunteers repel federal forces
Battle of Telford Depot, Tennessee
Federal troops reconquer the Cumberland Gap, Tennessee
New York Athletic Club forms
Netherlands and Britain sign "Koelietraktaat," an agreement allowing Indian contract workers from Calcutta to work on sugar plantations in Suriname
New York Gothams score 13 runs in an inning in a 16-6 win over Philadelphia Quakers at Recreation Park, Philadelphia; MLB record (18) set two days earlier
Northern Pacific Railroad's last spike is driven at Independence Creek, Montana
The first six Football League matches are played in England
First appearance of "Pledge of Allegiance" in Youth's Companion
British government sends an additional 10,000 troops to Natal, South Africa
6,000 people are killed when a hurricane and tidal wave strike Galveston, Texas
Pittsburgh Pirates strand National League record 18 men on base in 8-3 defeat to Cincinnati Reds at Exposition Park, Pittsburgh
Pope Pius X publishes the encyclical Pascendi Dominici Gregis (anti-modernism)
HMS (formerly RMS) Oceanic, two weeks into its service with the Royal Navy, runs aground off Foula, Shetland, Scotland, in good weather and eventually sinks
Private Thomas Highgate becomes the first British soldier to be executed for desertion during World War I
Association of Negro Life and History is founded in the US (now the Association for the Study of African American Life and History)
US Air Mail service begins (NYC to San Francisco)
Honda Point Disaster: Nine US Navy destroyers run aground off the California coast, and seven are lost
Alexandra Kollontai of Russia becomes the first woman ambassador
League of Nations Assembly votes unanimously to admit Germany
American inventor Richard Gurley Drew creates Scotch tape
First appearance of the comic strip "Blondie," created by American cartoonist Chic Young
New York City public schools begin teaching Hebrew
Luxury passenger ship Morro Castle, bound for New Jersey, catches fire; 133 die
Willy de Supervise swims a world record in the 200 m freestyle (2:25.2)
Pan-Arab conference on Palestine opens
General von Reichenau's Panzer division reaches the suburbs of Warsaw
Yankees beat Red Sox 4-1 in 7 innings; the game is called because of lightning
Blockade of Leningrad (St. Petersburg) by German forces begins
Entire Jewish community of Meretsch, Lithuania, is exterminated
NY Giants' pitcher Ace Adams sets a record by working in his 62nd game
First V-2 rockets land in London and Antwerp, Belgium
Russians march into Bulgaria, and Bulgaria declares war on Germany
US invades Japanese-held Korea
Bill Kennedy of Rocky Mount (CPL) strikes out minor league record 456
Bulgaria ends its monarchy
San Francisco 49ers play their first AAFC game and lose to the New York Yankees 21-7
The British de Havilland DH 108 fighter flies faster than the speed of sound
Japan signs a peace treaty with 48 countries in San Francisco
PopePius XIIpublishes the encyclical Fulgens corona
The South-East Asia Treaty Organization (SEATO) forms to stop the spread of communism in Southeast Asia
WINS radio station in NYC begins playing rock and roll music with Alan Freed's "Rock 'n' Roll Party" program
Earliest clinching of an NL pennant by the Brooklyn Dodgers
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical on motion pictures, radio, and television
Oman turns over Gwadar, on Balochistan coast, to Pakistan
The Asian Institute of Technology (AIT) is established
German 4 x 100 m relay team equals world record 39.5 to win the gold medal at Rome Olympics; Bernd Cullmann, Armin Hary, Walter Mahlendorf, and Martin Lauer
German Democratic Republic limits access to East Berlin for West Berliners
US 4 x 400 m relay team of Jack Yerman, Earl Young, Glenn Davis, and Otis Davis run a world record 3:02.2 to win the gold medal at the Rome Olympics
Nashville-based guitar wizard Hank Garland sustains career-ending injuries in a single vehicle accident near Springfield, Tennessee
Chinese troops exceed the McMahon Line (Tibet-India boundary)
Last run of the famous Pines Express over the Somerset and Dorset Railway line (UK) fittingly uses the last steam locomotive built by British Railways, 9F locomotive 92220 "Evening Star"
The USSR performs a nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya, USSR
Algerian population accepts the constitution
Inés Cuervo de Prieto, 34, gives birth to quintuplets, all boys, in Venezuela
Hurricane Betsy kills 75 in Louisiana and Florida
"That Girl," an American sitcom starring Marlo Thomas, premieres on ABC-TV
Star Trek premieres on television with the episode "The Man Trap"
Surveyor 5 launches and makes a soft landing on the Moon on September 10
The formal end of steam traction in the northeast of England by British Railways
Uganda abolishes traditional tribal kingdoms and becomes a republic
France performs a nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Saundra Williams wins the first Miss Black America pageant
Suleiman Maghrabi is appointed premier of Libya
Black September hijackings begin as three airliners are hijacked and destroyed by the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
John F. Kennedy Center for Performing Arts opens in Washington, D.C.
East German Annelie Ehrhardt sets a world record of 12.59 seconds to win the 100 m hurdles gold medal at the Munich Olympics, beating Romanian Valeria Bufanu by 0.25 seconds
Mykola Avilov, representing the Soviet Union, scores a world record 8,454 points to win the decathlon gold medal at the Munich Olympics, beating teammate Leonid Lytvynenko by 419 points
Runners Jim Ryun (US) and Billy Fordjour (Ghana) collide and fall in qualifying competitions for 1500 m finals, ending Ryun's chances for gold
"Star Trek: The Animated Series" premieres on TV
England's Ian Thompson wins European Championship marathon in 2:13:18.8 in Rome, Italy
Boston begins court-ordered busing of public schools
Fons Rademakers' film "Max Havelaar" premieres in Amsterdam
Interpol passes a resolution regarding video piracy
Jimmy McCulloch quits Wings
Second game of the Boston Massacre; NY Yankees beat the Red Sox 13-2
The US performs a nuclear test at the Nevada Test Site
British TV comedy "Only Fools and Horses," created by John Sullivan and starring David Jason, Nicholas Lyndhurst, and Lennard Pearce, premieres on BBC One
Dutch Social Democratic Party wins elections: far-right politician enters Dutch Parliament
NASA launches RCA-6
Challenger moves to the Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center in Florida for the mating of the STS-41G mission
Alayson Gibbons sets a 24-hour women's swim record of 42.05 miles in a 25-meter pool
Seven die in a car and train crash in San Jose, California
Shuttle Discovery flies back to Kennedy Space Center via Kelly Air Force Base
USA Weekend's first issue appears in 255 newspapers
Westinghouse sells the background music company Muzak to the Field Company of Chicago
Javier Sotomayor of Cuba high jumps a world record of 2.43 m
Mausoleum of Beatrice of Brabant (1288) is discovered in Kortrijk, Belgium
Norwegian Convair 580 crashes into the sea off Jutland, killing 55
Ellis Island Historical Site opens on Ellis Island, New York City
Macedonia votes for independence from Yugoslavia
NFL Buffalo Bills QB Jim Kelly passes for 6 touchdowns against the Pittsburgh Steelers (52-34)
Danny Tartabull has nine RBIs as the Yankees beat the Orioles 16-4
Howard Stern Radio Show begins broadcasting in Dallas, Texas, on KEGL-FM 97.1
Baseball's proposed switch to a three-division format is approved in the AL
Houston's Darryl Kile throws the third MLB no-hitter of the season in the Astros' 7-1 win over the New York Mets
Wang Junxia sets a women's world record 10,000 m (29:31.78) at the Chinese National Games
Last US, British, and French troops leave West Berlin
USAir Boeing 737 crashes at Pittsburgh Airport, killing all 132 on board
Cleveland Indians clinch first AL Central Division title
48th Emmy Awards: "ER," Dennis Franz, and Kathy Baker win
American children's program "Blue's Clues" debuts on Nickelodeon cable channel

US Attorney General Janet Reno names former Senator John Danforth to head an independent investigation of the 1993 fire at the Branch Davidian church near Waco, Texas, in response to revelations in the film "Waco The Rules of Engagement"

Rookie quarterback David Carr throws for 2 TDs as the Houston Texans beat the Dallas Cowboys 19-10 to become only the second expansion team, after the 1961 Minnesota Vikings, to win their inaugural game
Texas Rangers set a Major League record by hitting a home run in their 26th consecutive game as Texas falls to Tampa Bay
The NASA unmanned spacecraft Genesis crash-lands when its parachute fails to open
Two EMERCOM Il-76 aircraft land at a disaster aid staging area at Little Rock Air Force Base; it is the first time Russia has flown such a mission to North America

Latvia joins its northern neighbor Estonia in recession as GDP falls 0.2% in the second quarter from the first quarter, when it fell 0.3%; property markets and construction have suffered in both Baltic states
Egypt's Minister of Finance, Dr. Youssef Boutros Ghali, states that Egypt is emerging from the recession, as indicated by increased total revenue from sales taxes and customs duties
Fashion designer John Galliano is found guilty of making anti-Semitic remarks in a Paris court and is fined €6,000
A train hits a tractor in Romania, killing eight people
Six people are killed by a Taliban suicide bombing in Kabul, Afghanistan
11 people are killed and 18 are injured in a gunfire attack in Guatemala City
A minibus collides with a car in Jakarta, Indonesia, killing six people and injuring nine
A minibus collides with a train in Iasi, Romania, killing 11 people
Giraffe DNA study published in "Current Biology" reveals there are four species, not just one as previously assumed
NASA launches the OSIRIS-REx probe to retrieve a rock sample from the 500-meter-wide Bennu asteroid
75th Venice International Film Festival: a Netflix film, "Roma," wins the Golden Lion for the first time
Archaeologists uncover more than 300 gold coins from the late imperial period in a theatre in Como, Italy
Egypt sentences over 700 people, including 75 to death, for the 2013 pro-Muslim Brotherhood sit-in at Rabaa al-Adawiya Square
An estimated 1 million people attend a mass held byPope Francisnear Antananarivo, Madagascar
Brazil's Supreme Court rules that a Marvel comic depicting two men kissing can be sold after the mayor of Rio de Janeiro attempts to ban it
Cargo ship Golden Ray catches fire and capsizes in St. Simons Sound off the coast of Georgia, trapping four crew members inside
Typhoon Faxai makes landfall near Tokyo, Japan, with winds of up to 210 km/h (130 mph)
Moria refugee camp, Europe's largest migrant camp, burns down on the Greek island of Lesbos, leaving 13,000 without shelter
Two former Myanmar soldiers testify they were ordered to rape and kill Muslim Rohingya villagers, the first public confession of army-directed crimes against the Rohingya
Forty-one prisoners die in a fire at Tangerang prison, near Jakarta, Indonesia
Wildfire in Spain's Sierra Bermeja mountains in Andalucía erupts, forcing the evacuation of 2,600 people
Australian government passes its first climate change legislation in a decade, including cutting emissions by at least 43% by 2030
Australian Stephanie Gilmore wins her eighth world surfing title at Lower Trestles, California, making her the most successful women's champion in history
Europe has the warmest summer ever recorded, 0.4 degrees warmer, with August 0.8 degrees warmer, according to the Copernicus Climate Change Service

Ireland launches the Basic Income for the Arts (BIA) pilot scheme, providing €325 weekly payments to 2,000 randomly selected artists and creative arts workers to help the creative arts sector recover from the COVID-19 pandemic
Queen Elizabeth IIdies at Balmoral Castle after ruling for 70 years as the UK's longest-serving monarch; her eldest son inherits the throne as King Charles III
6.8 magnitude earthquake hits central Morocco, its epicenter in the Atlas Mountains near Marrakesh, killing around 3,000 people and causing catastrophic damage affecting over 6 million people
Fifty-nine people die in a crash between an oil tanker and a lorry carrying passengers in Niger State, Nigeria
Pope Franciscelebrates mass in the remote jungle town of Vanimo during his visit to Papua New Guinea
DukeConrad IIchosen as German king and crowned at Mainz Cathedral
French KingPhilip IVcaptures Pope Boniface VIII in Anagni
Charles Vabandons his siege of Peronne in northern France
Dutch privateerPiet Heyncaptures Spanish silver fleet in the Bay of Matanzas, Cuba
Battle of Bassano:Napoleon Bonaparte's French army defeats the Austrian forces during the French Revolutionary Wars
Abraham Lincolnsupposedly says in a speech "You may fool all the people some of the time; you can even fool some of the people all the time; but you can’t fool all of the people all the time"
Employed byThomas Edison, William K.L. Dickson films the first boxing match in West Orange, New Jersey, an exhibition between world heavyweight championJames J. Corbettand Peter Courtney
US PresidentWoodrow Wilsonsigns the Emergency Revenue Act, doubling the rate of income tax and adding inheritance and munitions profits taxes
Second Spanish government ofManuel Azañaforms
Princess Julianaof the Netherlands and German Prince Bernhard of Lippe-Biesterfeld become engaged
Cleveland Indians'Bob Feller, 20, is the youngest pitcher to win 20 games
Franklin D. Rooseveltdeclares a "limited national emergency" due to the war in Europe
Lieutenant-GeneralOmar Bradleyflies to Carthage, Tunisia, and then Algiers from Sicily to meet with Eisenhower and other senior Allied leaders

US GeneralDwight D. Eisenhowerand Italian Marshal and head of governmentPietro Badogliogive separate radio speeches announcing Italy's surrender to the Allies on September 3 in the Armistice of Cassibile
PopePius XIIpublishes the encyclical Sempiternus Rex
Ernest Hemingway's novel "The Old Man and the Sea" is published
With a 3-2 count, Phillies'Richie Ashburnfouls off the next 14 pitches, then walks
Harry Belafonte's album "Calypso" reaches #1 and stays #1 for 31 weeks
Jackie Wilsonreleases his first solo single "Reet Petite"
Juan Manuel Fangioconfirms his F1 World Drivers' Championship, finishing 2nd in the season-ending Italian GP at Monza; takes a record 5th title by 15 points fromStirling Moss
Paul Ankabegins his Asian tour in Tokyo
PirateRoberto Clementeties the modern record with three triples in a game
American sprinterWilma Rudolphwins her third gold medal of the Rome Olympics, anchoring the US 4 x 100 m relay team; she earlier won the 100/200 m double
Nationwide release ofAlfred Hitchcock's "Psycho" starringAnthony PerkinsandJanet Leigh
Milwaukee Braves' future Baseball Hall of Fame pitcherWarren SpahntiesChristy Mathewsonwith 13 20-win seasons
Scottish Lotus driverJim Clarkwins the Italian Grand Prix at Monza to claim his first F1 World Drivers' Championship, Clark's fifth GP win of the season
Kansas City A'sBert Campanerisplays all nine positions in a game
"Star Trek" premieres on NBC-TV starringWilliam ShatnerandLeonard Nimoy
The Severn Bridge between England and Wales is officially opened byQueen Elizabeth II
US amateur Men's Tennis:Stan Smithbeats Bob Lutz 9-7, 6-3, 6-1
Ferguson "Chic" Jenkins wins his 20th game for the sixth consecutive year
Billy Martinis named manager of the Texas Rangers
Hank Aaronsets a record for the most home runs in one league (733)
American motorcycle daredevilEvel Knievelattempts to jump the Snake River Canyon in Idaho but fails, escaping with minor injuries
US PresidentGerald Fordpardons former PresidentRichard Nixonof all federal crimes
The Iranian army fires onKhomeinifollowers protesting in Jaleh Square in Tehran, killing at least 100, an event that comes to be known as Black Friday
Bowie KuhnsuspendsFerguson Jenkinsindefinitely after being arrested in Toronto for possession of drugs
MLB Cincinnati Reds'Pete Roseties Ty Cobb with 4,191 hits
"TheOprah WinfreyShow" is first broadcast nationally
NL presidentA. Bartlett Giamattiis unanimously elected Major League Baseball's seventh Commissioner
American MLB playerGeorge Brettrecords his 2,500th hit for the Kansas City Royals against the Minnesota Twins
Island Records releases "Bone Machine," the 11th studio album by American singer-songwriterTom Waits; it earns him his first Grammy Award win for Best Alternative Music Album
TV series "Ally McBeal," starringCalista Flockhartand created by David E. Kelley, premieres on Fox in the US
"American Beauty," directed bySam Mendesand starringKevin Spacey,Annette Bening, andThora Birch, premieres in Los Angeles (Best Picture 2000)
Kylie Minoguereleases her single "Can't Get You Out of My Head," the biggest hit of her career
TV news show "TheRachel MaddowShow," hosted by Rachel Maddow and based on her radio show, premieres on MSNBC
Haider al-Abadibecomes the 75th Prime Minister of Iraq
ComedianStephen Colbertdebuts as the new host of CBS's "The Late Show"
Pope Francisannounces moves to streamline the annulment process within the Catholic Church
Smashing Pumpkins'Billy Corganannounces a new solo album, "Ogilala," recorded with producer Rick Rubin via a Facebook Live video; it is released on October 13, 2017
Derek Jeter, Ted Simmons, Larry Walker, and Marvin Miller are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, New York; elected in 2020, the ceremony was postponed due to the pandemic
Large statue of Confederate generalRobert E. Leeremoved from plinth on Monument Avenue, Richmond, Virginia
USBidenadministration releases blueprint calling for 44% of American electricity to come from the sun by 2050 (currently 3%)
FBI declassifies 270 pages of their surveillance files of American singerAretha Franklin
UK Prime MinisterLiz Trussannounces a significant support scheme to cap the price of energy bills at £2,500, costing up to £150 billion amid the cost of living crisis
Michelangelo's statue of David is unveiled in Florence
Siege of Leningrad, World War II, by German, Finnish, and eventually Spanish troops begins; battle lasts over 28 months as Soviets repel the invasion; well over a million lives are lost

Small ads in Daily Variety and Hollywood Reporter attract 437 young men interested in forming the world’s first manufactured boy band, "The Monkees." Three are chosen, with British actor-singerDavy Jonesalready cast
Julia Flavia, daughter of Roman Emperor Titus and lover of Domitian, born in Rome (d. 91)
Ansgar, German Catholic archbishop and Saint, born in Amiens, France (d. 865)
Ali al-Hadi, 10th Shia Imam, born in Medina, Abbasid Empire (d. 868)
Sancho II, King of Portugal (1223-47), born in Coimbra, Portugal (d. 1248)
Charles Martel of Anjou, Son of Charles II of Naples, born in Naples, Kingdom of Sicily (d. 1295)
Bernardino of Siena, Italian priest, Franciscan missionary, writer and Catholic saint, born in Massa Marittima, Italy (d. 1444)
Ludovico Ariosto, Italian poet (Orlando Furioso), born in Reggio Emilia, Italy (d. 1533)
Alfonso Salmeron, Spanish Jesuit biblical scholar, born in Toledo, Spain (d. 1585)
Marin Mersenne, French mathematician (Number of Mersenne), born in Oizé, Maine, France (d. 1648)
Jacqueline-Marie-Angélique Arnauld, French abbess (Port-Royal de Paris), born in Paris (d. 1661)
Johann Friedrich Gronovius, German classical scholar, born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1671)
Louis II Condé, Duke of Bourbon (Rocroy), born in Paris (d. 1686)
Ferdinand IV, King of the Romans, born in Vienna (d. 1654)
Nicolas de Grigny, French organist and composer, baptized in Reims, France (d. 1703)
François-Thomas-Marie de Baculard d'Arnaud, French sentimental writer (Les Épreuves du sentiment), born in Paris (d. 1805)
Bernardo Ottani, Italian composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (now Italy) (d. 1827)
Marie Thérèse Louise of Savoy, Princesse de Lamballe, French aristocrat, born in Palazzo Carignano, Turin, Savoy (d. 1792)
Yolande de Polastron, Duchess de Polignac, French aristocrat, favorite of Marie Antoinette at Versailles, born in Paris (d. 1793)
Carl Stenborg, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1813)
Anton Teyber, Austrian composer, born in Vienna (d. 1822)
August Wilhelm Schlegel, German poet, translator and critic, born in Hannover, Germany (d. 1845)
Karl August von Lichtenstein, German composer, born in Lahm, Bavaria (d. 1845)
Anna Katharina Emmerick, Roman Catholic Augustinian Canoness Regular of Windesheim, Marian visionary, ecstatic and stigmatist, born in Coesfeld, Westphalia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1824)
Clemens Brentano, German poet and author (The Story of the Just Casper and Fair Annie), born in Ehrenbreitstein, Germany (d. 1842)
Johann Philipp Samuel Schmidt, German composer, born in Königsberg, East Prussia (d. 1853)
Mustafa IV, 29th Sultan of Ottoman Empire (1807-08), born in Istanbul, Ottoman Empire (d. 1808)
N. F. S. Grundtvig, Danish writer and philosopher, born in Udby, Zealand, Denmark (d. 1872)
Joseph Netherclift, English composer (We Happy Shepherd Swains), and lithographer, born in the UK  (d. 1863)
Eduard Friedrich Mörike, German poet, born in Ludwigsburg, Germany (d. 1875)
Charles-Étienne Brasseur de Bourbourg, French writer and historian, born in Bourbourg, France (d. 1874)
Alexander Ramsey, American politician (1st Governor of Minnesota Territory), born in Hummelstown, Pennsylvania (d. 1903)
Henry Baxter, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Sidney, New York (d. 1873)
Jaime Nunó, Spanish composer (Mexican national anthem), born in Sant Joan de les Abadesses, Catalonia (d. 1908)
Disma Fumagalli, Italian composer, born in Inzago, Milan, Italy (d. 1893)
Emil Naumann, German composer, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (now Germany) (d. 1888)
Joshua Lawrence Chamberlain, American professor and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Brewer, Maine (d. 1914)
George Crook, American Major General (Union Army), born in Taylorsville, Ohio (d. 1890)
Seth Barton, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Fredericksburg, Virginia (d. 1900)
Wilhelm Raabe [Jakob Corvinus], German author (Der Hungerpastor), born in Eschershausen, Germany (d. 1910)
Gojong, Emperor Gwangmu of Korea (1897-1907), born in Unhyeon Palace, Hanseong, Joseon dynasty, Korea (d. 1919)
Willem Marinus van Rossum, Dutch cardinal and prefect of Propaganda File, born in Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands (d. 1932)
Georg Michaelis, German lawyer and politician (Chancellor of German Empire and Prime Minister of Prussia, July-November 1917), born in Haynau, Germany (d. 1936)

Ida Henrietta Hyde, American physiologist who invented the micro-electrode and the first woman allowed to do research at Harvard Medical School and to be elected to the American Physiology Society, born in Davenport, Iowa (d. 1945)
Gustavo Campa, Mexican composer, born in Mexico City (d. 1934)
Adolfo Albertazzi, Italian writer (Amore & Amore, Top), born in Bologna, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1924)
Hermann Hans Wetzler, German-American composer, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1943)
Alfred Jarry, French writer (Ubu Roi), born in Layal, Mayenne, Paris (d. 1907)
David O. McKay, 9th President of Latter-day Saints, born in Huntsville, Utah (d. 1970)
Roelof Kranenburg, Dutch social democratic chairman (1st Chamber), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1956)
Harry Hillman, American athlete (Olympic gold 400m, 200/400m hurdles 1904; silver 400m hurdles 1908), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1945)
Théodore Pilette, Belgian auto racer (first Belgian Indy 500), born in Brussels, Belgium (d. 1921)
Siegfried Sassoon, English poet and writer (Counterattack), born in Matfield, Kent, England (d. 1967)
George, Crown Prince of Serbia, eldest son of King Peter I, born in Cetinje, Montenegro (d. 1972)
Louis Zimmer, Flemish astronomer and clockmaker (Jubelklok/Wonder Clock), born in Lier, Belgium (d. 1970)
Robert A. Taft, American politician (U.S. Senator from Ohio), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1953)
Willem Pijper, Dutch composer and music critic, born in Zeist, Netherlands (d. 1947)
Sara García, Mexican actress, born in Orizaba, Veracruz, Mexico (d. 1980)
Elmer Schoebel, American jazz pianist, arranger (New Orleans Rhythm Kings; Isham Jones), and composer ("Farewell Blues"; "Prince Of Wails"), born in East St. Louis, Illinois (d. 1970)
Howard Dietz, American lyricist and librettist ("Dancing In The Dark"; "I Guess I'll Have To Change My Plans"), born in New York City (d. 1983)
Queenie Smith, American character actress and dancer (Funny Side; Little House On The Prairie), born in Texas (d. 1978)
Claude Pepper, American politician (Rep-D-FL, 1963-89), born in Dudleyville, Chambers County, Alabama (d. 1989)
Gavin Muir, American actor (Mary of Scotland, Johnny Trouble), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1972)
Hendrik Verwoerd, South African Prime Minister (1958-66) who was assassinated in 1966, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1966)
Nico Donkersloot, Dutch literary and writer, born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1965)
Carlos Sánchez Málaga, Peruvian composer, born in Arequipa, Perú (d. 1995)
Henry Wilcoxon, British actor (Cleopatra, Jericho, The Ten Commandments), born in Dominica, British West Indies (d. 1984)
Andrei Kirilenko, Russian member of Central Committee of Communist Party, born in Alexeyevka, Voronezh Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1990)
Buck Leonard, American Baseball HOF first baseman (Negro League World Series 1943, 44, 48 Homestead Grays; 13 x NL All Star), born in Rocky Mount, North Carolina (d. 1997)
Leon Askin, Austrian actor (One, Two, Three, Charlie Farrell Show), born in Vienna, Austria-Hungary (d. 2005)
Sid Tomack, American actor (The Life of Riley, Adventures of Superman), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1962)
Geoffrey Hodges, British bomb disposal expert, born in Broughton, England (d. 1996)
Johnny Lindsay, South African cricket wicketkeeper (3 Tests, 5 dismissals; North Eastern Transvaal), born in Barkly East, South Africa (d. 1990)
Gella Allaert, Flemish actress (La Round), born in Oostende, Belgium (d. 2002)
Jean-Louis Barrault, French stage actor, director and producer (Les Enfants du Paradis), born in Le Vésinet, Seine-et-Oise, France (d. 1994)
Alexander Mackendrick, American-born Scottish director (Man in the White Suit, Mandy), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1993)
Alfio Grasso, Italian jazz guitarist and composer, born in Lentini, Sicily, Italy (d. 1962)
Leo Cherne, American economist and commentator (All Star News), born in New York City (d. 1999)
Dave Bowman, American jazz and studio pianist (Jack Teagarden; Bud Freeman; Perry Como), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1964)
Dimitrios I, Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople (1973-91), born in Constantinople, Ottoman Empire (d. 1991)
Doug Freeman, New Zealand cricket spin bowler (2 Tests, 1 wicket; Wellington), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1994)
Hillary Brooke, American film actress (The Abbott and Costello Show), born in Astoria, New York (d. 1999)
Frank Cady, American actor (The Beverly Hillbillies; Petticoat Junction; Green Acres - all as "Sam Drucker"), born in Susanville, California (d. 2012)
Frank Pullen, English businessman and racehorse owner, born in London (d. 1992)
N. V. M. González, Filipino novelist, short story writer, and, poet, born in Romblon, Philippines (d. 1999)
Derek Barton, British organic chemist (Nobel Prize for Chemistry 1969), born in Gravesend, Kent (d. 1998)[1]
John F. Seiberling, American politician (Rep-D-OH, 1971-86), born in Akron, Ohio (d. 2008)
Sarah Cunningham, American actress (Trapper John, M.D. - "Nurse Andrews"), born in Greenville, South Carolina (d. 1986)
Gianni Brera, Italian sports journalist and writer, born in San Zenone al Po, Italy (d. 1992)
Johan Kvandal, Norwegian composer, born in Kristiania, Norway (d. 1999)
Hans Ulrich Engelmann, German composer (Ophelia), born in Darmstadt, Germany (d. 2011)
Harry Secombe, Welsh actor, comedian, singer and goon (The Goon Show; Oliver!), born in Swansea, Wales (d. 2001)
Norris Turney, American jazz flute and saxophone player (Billy Eckstine, 1947-48; Duke Ellington Orchestra, 1968-73), born in Wilmington, Ohio (d. 2001)
Isaac Sidney "Sid" Caesar, American comic actor and writer (Your Show of Shows), born in Yonkers, New York (d. 2014)
